bio.bio_blurb: Director of Harvard’s Fogg Museum for twenty years

notable.notable_blurb: Fogg Museum director; MFA Boston president
notable.primary_url_label: American Academy of Arts and Sciences

narrative_blocks.header: He gave up architecture for its history
narrative_blocks.body: Coolidge moved to New York to become an architect, then decided he lacked the talent and switched to architectural history under the émigré scholars Panofsky and Krautheimer. His first book on the Lowell mill town became a classic of American social history.

Named director of Harvard’s Fogg Art Museum at thirty-four, Coolidge ran it for two decades, strengthening its Islamic and modern holdings and adding the museum’s first modernist works. He treated the museum as a laboratory for training future curators.

narrative_blocks.header: He roller-skated between his train commutes
narrative_blocks.body: A co-founder of the Society of Architectural Historians, Coolidge later presided over the Museum of Fine Arts in Boston. He was known to roller-skate between stations on his Princeton-to-New-York commute.

cross_connections.display_label: gave his entire art collection to the Fogg, which this man directed

bio.bio_blurb: Harvard mathematician; first master of Lowell House

notable.notable_blurb: Harvard geometer; first master of Lowell House

narrative_blocks.header: He taught a future president at Groton
narrative_blocks.body: Before joining Harvard, Coolidge taught at Groton, where one of his pupils was Franklin Delano Roosevelt; the two remained friends for life. He went on to spend his whole career at Harvard, chairing its mathematics department.

narrative_blocks.header: He held Oxford’s first science degree
narrative_blocks.body: After Harvard, Coolidge took the very first Bachelor of Science ever awarded by Oxford, in 1897, then a doctorate at Bonn under Eduard Study. His treatises on the circle, the sphere, and non-Euclidean geometry became standards.

narrative_blocks.header: He built Lowell House from raw brick
narrative_blocks.body: Named the first master of Lowell House in 1930, Coolidge had to invent the residential culture of a building “new and raw from the builders’ hands.” His vision shaped Harvard’s house system for generations.

narrative_blocks.header: A geometer who commanded two thousand men
narrative_blocks.body: During the First World War, Coolidge served as a major and liaison officer to the French General Staff, with two thousand American men under his command in Paris. France made him a chevalier of the Legion of Honor in 1919.

narrative_blocks.header: In retirement he wrote mathematics’ history
narrative_blocks.body: After retiring in 1940, Coolidge turned to the history of his field, publishing studies of geometrical methods and "The Mathematics of Great Amateurs." He was a record-setting miler in his Oxford days and a lifelong oarsman.

cross_connections.display_label: a fellow eminent American mathematics professor of the era

bio.bio_blurb: Linguist; matriarch of a Harvard academic dynasty

narrative_blocks.header: Her marriage seeded a Harvard dynasty
narrative_blocks.body: A linguist by training, Theresa Reynolds married the mathematician Julian Lowell Coolidge in 1901 and bore seven children. Among them were a Fogg Museum director and a daughter who married Boston’s leading cultural historian.
